text: The surgeon inserts a surgical device into the port and connects it, preparing to assess the bone structure. The right side of the spleen is visually examined.
The surgeon has completely separated the rich membrane, ensuring that the function of the nervous system is maintained during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon inserts a surgical mesh into the abdomen, using a machine arm equipped with an anti-seizure band to position the mesh accurately.

The surgeon removes the thread and needle from the surgical site and introduces a new thread and needle head into the body. The second needle is sewn under the hip bone, and the thread is cut off and removed.
The surgeon uses their fingers to press on the muscles at the base of the pelvis and lower back, confirming the position and stability of the pelvis to prevent it from falling.

The surgeon applies the second dose deep into the tissue to strengthen the bone marrow. After completing the sewing line, the sewing needle and line are removed from the surgical field. The third needle is positioned on the left side of the bone and at the bottom of the muscle.
The surgeon uses a non-discriminatory sewing line to secure the webbing onto the lower bone, focusing on the area where the center of the bone is raised.
The surgeon cuts additional thread to remove the knot and then manually removes the excess fabric from the surgical area.

The surgeon inserts a V-LOOK-SARMAPS sewing needle, threading the V-LOOK sewing thread through a small hole at the end. The sewing thread appears slightly crooked, allowing it to move in one direction without needing to be tied when the seam is tight.
